title: Fitting a cistern displacement device
subtitle: A save-a-flush bag or Hippo displaces 1-3 litres per flush
excerpt: A cistern displacement device sits inside the toilet cistern and takes up space that would otherwise be water, reducing the flush volume by 1-3 litres per flush without affecting flush performance. Fitting takes under five minutes and costs nothing, water companies in England and Wales post them free on request.
sourceNotes: Water UK Save-a-Flush programme documentation. Waterwise household water savings data. WRAP water efficiency guidance.

content.text: A standard pre-1990s toilet cistern holds 9-13 litres per flush. Modern dual-flush cisterns use 4 litres (reduced) or 6 litres (full flush), but older single-flush cisterns are common in pre-1990s homes. A Save-a-Flush bag, Hippo bag, or similar device placed inside the cistern displaces 1-3 litres per fill, reducing each flush volume without any plumbing work.

Most water companies in England and Wales post displacement devices free to metered and unmetered customers. Check your water company's website, search for 'water saving devices'. Waterwise.org.uk also lists suppliers. If ordering from a water company, devices typically arrive within one week.

Lift the lid of the toilet cistern. Check the cistern has at least 6 litres of water when full, look for the water level relative to the overflow pipe. A cistern with less than 6 litres may not flush adequately with a displacement device fitted.

For a Save-a-Flush bag: fill it with water at the tap and seal. The bag sits in the cistern full of water, taking up volume. For a Hippo bag: fill with water via the filling valve on top, then clip to the inside of the cistern. For a rigid plastic device: fill with water through the top and place upright in the cistern.

Flush the toilet and watch that the bowl clears fully. If the flush performance is reduced, the bowl does not clear solids, remove the device. Some older cisterns with low water volume cannot accommodate displacement without affecting performance.

At 5 flushes per person per day and a 2-litre displacement per flush: 5 × 2 × 365 = 3,650 litres per person per year. At a metered water rate of approximately .50 per cubic metre combined supply and sewerage: saving = 3.65 × .50 = .50/year per person. Not large, but free and takes five minutes.
